figures.gloss: The 8th-century Babylonian sage who rejected the Talmud's authority and issued his own Aramaic Book of Commandments (Sefer ha-Mitzvot) drawn from Scripture alone; later Karaite tradition crowned him founder of the movement.
figures.facet: A founder made in retrospect: scholarship distinguishes Anan's rigorist Ananites from the Karaites proper who absorbed them a century later — the 'Anan founded Karaism' story is the community's own back-formation, carried here as such.
figures.provenance: Al-Qirqisani, Kitab al-Anwar (937), the earliest inside account; Nemoy's Karaite Anthology; the founder-critique in Moshe Gil and Yoram Erder. The famous dictum 'Search well in Scripture, and lean not on my opinion' is attributed to him only in later tradition.

figures.gloss: The written Tanakh as the sole binding authority — the movement's very name comes from mikra, 'that which is read.' The Mishnah and Talmud are human commentary, revered by the rabbis but never revealed at Sinai.
figures.facet: The cleanest test-case of what happens when a revealed text is stripped of its interpretive guild: each Karaite is obligated to derive the law personally, and the community lived with the resulting plurality.
figures.provenance: Karaite legal literature from Benjamin al-Nahawandi (9th c.) onward; surveyed in Meira Polliack (ed.), Karaite Judaism: A Guide to Its History and Literary Sources (2003).

figures.gloss: The ascetic Karaite community that answered Daniel al-Qumisi's 9th-century call to leave the diaspora and settle Jerusalem — fasting, keeping night-vigils, and weeping over the ruined Temple to hasten the redemption.
figures.facet: Mourning weaponized into eschatology: grief for the lost center performed AT the center, on the conviction that the exile persists only because the exiled have not returned to weep in place.
figures.provenance: Daniel al-Qumisi's epistle urging settlement of Jerusalem; the Jerusalem Karaite corpus studied by Yoram Erder (The Karaite Mourners of Zion) and in Cairo Geniza documents.

figures.gloss: The 10th-century systematizer whose Kitab al-Anwar wal-Maraqib (937) codified Karaite law and opened with the earliest surviving comparative survey of Jewish sects — rabbinic, Karaite, and vanished alike.
figures.facet: The dissident as the tradition's best historian: because Karaism had to justify its split, it produced the map of Jewish diversity that the majority had no reason to draw.
figures.provenance: Kitab al-Anwar wal-Maraqib, ed. Nemoy (1939-43); treated throughout Polliack's Guide.

figures.gloss: Months begun at the sighted new crescent and the year intercalated by inspecting the ripeness (aviv) of barley in the Land of Israel — the observational calendar kept against the rabbis' fixed calculation.
figures.facet: The scripture-only principle made agricultural: rather than trust Hillel II's computed calendar, the Karaite year is re-anchored every spring to a moon actually seen and a crop actually checked.
figures.provenance: Karaite halakhic literature on Exodus 12 and Deuteronomy 16; the practice is maintained by the Karaite community in Israel today, with annual aviv inspections.

figures.gloss: The plateau fortress-town near Bakhchysarai that became the heartland of the Turkic-speaking Crimean Karaites (Qaraylar), with its kenesas and the venerated oak cemetery of Balta Tiymez — 'the axe does not touch.'
figures.facet: Where a scriptural movement grew a folk landscape: sacred oaks over ancestral graves in a community that elsewhere prided itself on textual purism — and where 19th-century identity politics (Firkovich's doctored antiquities, the contested Khazar-descent theory) redrew who the Qaraylar said they were.
figures.provenance: Abraham Firkovich's manuscript collections (his tombstone-date forgeries exposed by Harkavy); modern treatments in Polliack's Guide and studies of the Qaraylar; the Khazar-descent claim is carried here as a contested 19th-century construction, not accepted history.

figures.gloss: 9th-century Persian-Jewish scholar (Nahawand) whose legal writings consolidated scripturalism a generation after Anan ben David; later sources credit him as the first to use the name 'Bene Miqra' (Karaites) for the movement, and he taught that the world was created and is administered by an intermediary angel rather than by God directly.
figures.facet: The rationalist edge of early scripturalism: guarding divine transcendence by relocating direct creative agency to a subordinate angelic power — a solution the later Karaite mainstream did not keep, but that marks the movement's earliest theological experimentation.
figures.provenance: Summarized in al-Qirqisani's Kitab al-Anwar (937); discussed in Nemoy's Karaite Anthology and Meira Polliack (ed.), Karaite Judaism: A Guide to Its History and Literary Sources (2003).

figures.gloss: Late-9th-century Karaite leader from Damghan (Persia) who repudiated Anan's authority, urged return to Scripture without any intermediary founder-figure, and himself settled in Jerusalem; his epistle summoning the diaspora to mourn at the Temple ruins launched the Mourners of Zion.
figures.facet: The mourning turned migratory: al-Qumisi converts textual scripturalism into a physical return-and-vigil program, relocating the movement's center of gravity from Babylonia to Jerusalem for good.
figures.provenance: Al-Qumisi's own epistle and eschatological writings, analyzed in Yoram Erder, The Karaite Mourners of Zion (2004); Polliack's Guide.

figures.gloss: 10th-century Jerusalem exegete who produced a complete Judeo-Arabic translation and running commentary on the entire Tanakh — the fullest scripture-alone program ever carried out by a Karaite scholar, read even by rabbanite opponents such as Abraham ibn Ezra, who cites him repeatedly.
figures.facet: Puts mikra into practice verse by verse: where the movement's founding principle is scripture alone, Yefet is the one scholar who actually finished the task across the whole canon.
figures.provenance: Yefet ben Eli's Judeo-Arabic Bible commentaries, partial editions and studies surveyed in Polliack's Guide (2003).

figures.gloss: Nicomedia scholar (c. 1328-1369) whose trilogy — Etz Hayyim (philosophy), Gan Eden (law), and Keter Torah (exegesis) — gave Byzantine Karaism its first comprehensive theological summa, written in deliberate dialogue with Maimonides.
figures.facet: The systematic mirror to Karaism's most-cited rabbanite antagonist: proof that golden-age scripturalism could scale into full philosophical theology, not only legal ruling.
figures.provenance: Etz Hayyim, Gan Eden, Keter Torah (14th c.); surveyed in Polliack's Guide and Daniel Lasker's studies of Karaite-rabbanite philosophical polemic.

figures.gloss: Constantinople scholar (c. 1420-1490) whose law code Adderet Eliyahu became the standard practical legal authority for Karaite communities, notably accepting a pre-calculated calendar for diaspora communities unable to inspect Land-of-Israel barley and easing the Sabbath-light prohibition.
figures.facet: The closing codification the legal story otherwise lacks: where the aviv calendar holds to direct observation in the Land, Bashyazi's code makes the diaspora-workable compromise official.
figures.provenance: Adderet Eliyahu (printed edition, Gözleve 1835); discussed in Polliack's Guide and studies of Ottoman-era Karaite law.

figures.gloss: The Karaite principle that consensus practice transmitted from the fathers binds where Scripture itself is silent — an acknowledged non-scriptural authority, defended as inherited practice rather than as a rival revelation.
figures.facet: The honest complication inside scripture-alone: mikra never specifies how gaps in the law get filled in daily life, and sevel ha-yerushah is the community's own admission that inherited custom, not private exegesis alone, closes them.
figures.provenance: Karaite legal literature on sevel ha-yerushah, discussed in Polliack's Guide and standard surveys of Karaite halakha (e.g., Daniel Lasker, Zvi Ankori).

figures.gloss: Crimean Karaite hakham and manuscript collector (1787-1874) whose expeditions across Crimea, the Caucasus, and the Middle East assembled the Firkovich Collections — now in the National Library of Russia, St Petersburg — the largest single trove of Karaite and Genizah-era manuscripts ever gathered.
figures.facet: A double legacy carried honestly: the same expeditions that preserved irreplaceable manuscripts also produced doctored tombstone dates and colophons, later exposed by Abraham Harkavy, that underwrote the contested claim of Crimean Karaite descent from the Khazars rather than from Jews proper.
figures.provenance: Firkovich's own collection catalogs; Harkavy's 19th-century exposure of the forged dates; discussed in Polliack's Guide and in studies of the Qaraylar identity question.

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: The same Written Torah, split over the Oral — Karaism accepts the rabbis' scripture and rejects their Mishnah-Talmud chain of authority; a child of Judaism defined by the refusal.
GOLD SEAM: The Mourners settled 10th-century Jerusalem in ascetic grief expressly to hasten the messianic redemption both communities await — one eschatology across the rabbanite-Karaite split.

GOLD SEAM: Scripture-alone raised against an authorized interpretive tradition — the 'Protestants of Judaism' label is a later comparative analogy; Karaism precedes the Reformation by seven centuries and no contact is claimed.

GOLD SEAM: Two refusals of the rabbinic chain — but Samaritans keep only their own Pentateuch while Karaites keep the full Tanakh; independent splits (medieval polemics linking Karaites to the Sadducees are contested, not descent).

Cross-tradition seam, held low: both are minority movements that split from their mainstream by insisting an older received form (pre-reform ritual practice; scripture alone against rabbinic tradition) is the only legitimate one — a structural rhyme of schism-through-textual-purism, though the content of each dispute differs sharply.
Al-Qumisi's own epistle is the founding act that convened the Mourners of Zion — leader and movement, one event.
Yefet's complete Judeo-Arabic Bible commentary is scripture-alone put into practice across the whole canon, not merely asserted as principle.

Two systematizers four centuries apart, each giving Karaism a comprehensive account of itself at a moment of external pressure — al-Qirqisani mapping Jewish sectarian diversity, Aaron ben Elijah answering Maimonides.
Bashyazi's code is the diaspora-workable compromise on the same observational calendar principle: fixed calculation permitted where direct aviv inspection in the Land of Israel is impossible.
The internal correction to pure scripturalism: an acknowledged non-scriptural authority operating alongside 'scripture alone,' defended as inheritance rather than revelation.
Firkovich's collecting and his contested tombstone chronology are what made Chufut-Kale the flashpoint of 19th-century Crimean Karaite identity politics.

dossier.essence: A Jewish movement, crystallizing in the Abbasid East from the 8th-9th centuries, that holds the written Tanakh alone to be binding revelation and rejects the rabbinic Oral Torah — the Mishnah and Talmud — as human commentary never given at Sinai. Its golden age was 10th-11th-century Jerusalem; living communities survive today, centered on Israel (notably Ramla) with smaller diasporas, alongside the distinct Turkic-speaking Crimean Karaites.
dossier.cosmos: Karaite theology is broadly that of biblical Judaism: one incorporeal God who created the world, revealed the Torah at Sinai, and judges humanity, with the messianic redemption and ingathering to Zion awaited. What sets the movement apart is not cosmology but epistemology — revelation ends at the written text, and each Karaite is personally obligated to derive the law from Scripture by reason and study rather than receive it from a rabbinic chain of authority.
dossier.practice: Law drawn straight from Scripture diverges from rabbinic practice at fixed points: months begin at the sighted new crescent and the year is intercalated by inspecting the ripeness (aviv) of barley in the Land of Israel; tefillin are not worn, the proof-verses being read figuratively; Sabbath and purity rules run stricter in the classical tradition. Worship centers on the kenesa, with prostration and a liturgy woven largely from biblical text; the medieval Mourners of Zion added ascetic vigils and fasting in Jerusalem to hasten redemption.
dossier.canon: The Tanakh alone is binding; the movement's own classics — Anan ben David's Aramaic Sefer ha-Mitzvot, al-Qirqisani's Kitab al-Anwar wal-Maraqib (937), and a large Judeo-Arabic exegetical and legal corpus — carry authority as commentary, never as revelation.
dossier.reflects: What becomes of a revealed text when its interpretive guild is refused — sola scriptura lived out in Judaism seven centuries before the Reformation, with the burden of reading placed on every believer.
sources: Leon Nemoy, 'Karaite Anthology: Excerpts from the Early Literature' (1952)
Meira Polliack (ed.), 'Karaite Judaism: A Guide to Its History and Literary Sources' (2003)
Yoram Erder, 'The Karaite Mourners of Zion and the Qumran Scrolls' (2017)

links.cite: Leon Nemoy, 'Karaite Anthology: Excerpts from the Early Literature' (1952)
